title: Prepayment meter vs direct debit: the cost difference
description: Is prepayment dearer than direct debit in 2026? How the Ofgem cap aligned the rates, what still costs more, and how to work out your own difference.
blurb: Prepay used to cost more than direct debit. Here is what the gap really is in 2026 and why.
lede: For years the rule of thumb was simple: if you paid for your gas and electricity through a prepayment meter, you paid more than someone on monthly direct debit. That gap was real, it was unfair, and it hit the households least able to absorb it. Since 2024 the Ofgem price cap has closed most of it, so the per-unit cost is now broadly the same and prepayment is actually a touch cheaper at typical use. The catch is that headline rates are not the whole story, and direct debit still carries one quiet advantage that prepay cannot match.
short_answer: Under the Ofgem cap from 1 July 2026, prepayment unit rates and standing charges are set at the same level as direct debit, and at typical use a prepay dual-fuel bill works out slightly lower (around £1,812 a year versus £1,862 for direct debit). The old prepay premium of roughly £100 a year is gone. The remaining gap is not in the unit price but in cash flow, fixed deals and debt: direct debit smooths winter costs and gives access to cheaper fixed tariffs that prepay customers cannot always get.

sections.html:
The Ofgem price cap sets a maximum unit rate and standing charge for each payment method. Until 2024, prepayment customers were quietly charged more per unit than direct debit customers to reflect the supplier's cost of running the prepay system. Ofgem ended that with a policy called levelisation, which spreads the cost across all direct debit bills instead. The result is that prepayment standing charges and unit rates are now held at the same level as the direct debit cap, and at typical consumption the total prepay bill comes out marginally lower.

Here is how a typical dual-fuel household compares under the cap running from 1 July to 30 September 2026.

Payment methodElectricity unitGas unitTypical annual bill
Direct debit26.11p/kWh7.33p/kWh£1,862
PrepaymentSame as direct debitSame as direct debit£1,812
Standard credit (pay on receipt of bill)Higher than direct debitHigher than direct debitHighest of the three

Direct debit rates: Ofgem cap July to September 2026, 26.11p/kWh electricity and 7.33p/kWh gas, standing charges 57.19p and 29.04p per day. Typical-use figures are Ofgem's annual estimates for a medium dual-fuel home.

The pattern that matters is the order. Prepayment is now the cheapest of the three at typical use, direct debit sits just above it, and the most expensive way to pay is standard credit, where you receive a bill and pay it manually. If you are choosing between those three, prepay and direct debit are close, and standard credit is the one to avoid.

sections.h2: Why prepayment used to cost more
sections.html:
The historic premium came from two places. First, suppliers said it cost more to operate prepayment meters, top-up networks and customer support, so they loaded that onto prepay tariffs. Second, prepay customers were locked out of the cheapest fixed deals, which were almost all direct debit only. Together those could add roughly £100 a year, and the people affected were disproportionately on low incomes.

Ofgem's levelisation fix moved the operating cost off prepay bills and onto a small charge spread across direct debit customers. That is why direct debit standing charges now carry a few pence a day that prepay does not. It is a deliberate cross-subsidy, designed so the cheapest-to-serve payment method helps fund fairer pricing for a group that was being penalised. You can see exactly where standing charges go in our guide to standing charges, and how the cap itself is built in how the energy price cap works.

sections.h2: The real difference is cash flow, not unit price
sections.html:
With unit rates now level, the genuine difference between the two methods is about when and how you pay, not how much each kilowatt hour costs.

Direct debit spreads the cost

A monthly direct debit averages your annual usage into twelve roughly equal payments. You pay the same in July as in January even though you burn far more energy in winter. That smoothing is the single biggest practical advantage of direct debit. It stops a brutal winter bill landing all at once, and it builds a credit balance over summer that covers the cold months.

Prepayment makes you pay as you go

A prepay meter charges you in real time. You top up, you use the energy, the meter counts down. There is no smoothing, so your costs spike in winter exactly when money is tightest. The upside is control and visibility: you cannot run up a surprise debt, and you can see precisely what you are spending. For some households that certainty is worth more than the averaging direct debit offers. A smart meter running in prepay mode gives you that same control without the trek to a shop to top up.

The hidden cost of self-disconnection

Prepay's quiet danger is self-disconnection. If the credit runs out, the supply stops. Some households end up rationing heat or going without rather than topping up, which is a real cost that never shows on any tariff comparison. Direct debit cannot cut you off the same way, because the supply keeps running and any shortfall is settled later.

sections.h2: Fixed deals and switching: where direct debit still wins
sections.html:
The cap sets a ceiling, not the cheapest price available. Below the cap sit fixed tariffs that lock in a unit rate for a year or more, and the most competitive of these are still aimed at direct debit customers. Prepay customers have fewer fixed options and they tend not to be the market-leading ones, so a confident direct debit customer who shops around can get further below the cap than a prepay customer typically can.

That is the strongest remaining argument for direct debit: not the standard cap rate, where prepay is fractionally cheaper, but the access to better fixed deals underneath it. If you are weighing a fix against the cap, read fixed vs variable tariffs first, then run your own numbers using how to switch suppliers. If you pay by prepay and want the cheaper fixes, you generally need to move to direct debit first, which usually means clearing any meter debt and passing a credit check.

Headline annual figures assume an average home. Yours is not average, so do the sum on your own usage. The method is the same whichever way you pay.

  • Find your annual kilowatt hours for gas and electricity from a recent bill or your in-home display.
  • Multiply electricity kWh by 0.2611 and gas kWh by 0.0733 to get your usage cost in pounds.
  • Add standing charges: 57.19p plus 29.04p per day is 86.23p a day, roughly £315 a year for dual fuel.
  • That total is your direct debit cap cost. For prepay at the same unit rates, the result is essentially the same, with prepay coming out very slightly lower at typical use.

Because the unit numbers match, the comparison rarely turns on the maths. It turns on the questions the maths cannot answer: can you absorb a big winter bill, do you risk self-disconnecting, and can you access a fixed deal? If you want to read your meter accurately for this, see how to read your energy meter.

In 2026 there is no longer a meaningful unit-price penalty for prepayment. Ofgem's levelisation put prepay rates level with direct debit, and at typical use prepay is the cheaper of the two by a small margin, with standard credit the dearest way to pay. The old £100-ish prepay premium is history.

So the choice now comes down to your circumstances rather than the tariff sheet. Choose prepayment if you value hard spending limits, want to avoid debt, and can handle higher winter top-ups without going without heat. Choose direct debit if you want winter costs smoothed across the year and you intend to chase the cheaper fixed deals that sit below the cap. Both are legitimate. The wrong move is standard credit, and the genuinely costly mistake is self-disconnecting on prepay to save money that the cap has already handed back to you. Prepay tariffs, schemes and protections change, so check the current rates and the rules on forced installation on the Ofgem website before you decide.

faq.q: Is a prepayment meter more expensive than direct debit in 2026?
faq.a: No. Since Ofgem levelised the rates in 2024, prepayment unit rates and standing charges are held at the same level as direct debit. At typical use a prepay dual-fuel bill is actually slightly lower, around £1,812 a year versus £1,862 for direct debit under the cap from 1 July 2026. The old prepay premium of roughly £100 a year no longer applies.

faq.q: Why does direct debit still get cheaper deals then?
faq.a: The cap is a maximum, not the cheapest price. Fixed tariffs that sit below the cap are mostly aimed at direct debit customers, so a direct debit household that shops around can get further below the cap than a prepay household usually can. The advantage is access to cheaper fixes, not the standard cap rate.

faq.q: Can I switch from prepayment to direct debit?
faq.a: Usually yes. You normally need to clear any debt held on the meter and pass a credit check, after which the supplier can swap the meter to credit mode or change the billing on a smart meter. Once on direct debit you can access the wider range of fixed tariffs. Contact your supplier to start the process.

faq.q: What is self-disconnection and why does it matter?
faq.a: Self-disconnection is when a prepay meter runs out of credit and the supply stops, so the household goes without gas or electricity until they top up. It is a hidden cost that never shows on a tariff comparison, because people may ration heat to avoid it. Direct debit cannot cut you off the same way, as the supply keeps running and any shortfall is settled later.

faq.q: Which is the most expensive way to pay for energy?
faq.a: Standard credit, where you receive a bill and pay it manually after using the energy. Under the Ofgem cap standard credit carries higher unit rates and standing charges than both direct debit and prepayment, so it is the one to move away from if you can.
